The thunder roared and the
lightning flashed for an hour or so
last night and as a result only a
small crowd heard Gov. Mead and
Congressman Jones at the labor
temple carnival. Mayor Moore in
troduced Gov. Mead as a man who
had made good In the governors
chair and the governor devoted his
address largely to administration
matters. especial interest to citi-
zens generally.


He outlined the history of union
ism as one of the remarkable and
beneficial developments Of the past
80 years. AS the surest and safety
remedies for differences between
labor and capital he advocated ar
bitration.


In recapitulating his administra-
ton Gov. Mead said that all the
demands of the republican plat
forms of 1904 and 1906 had been
carried into effect and important
legislation enacted not embodied In
the platform, among the latter be-
ing the railroad commission, for
which the platform failed to de-
clare. The governor, previous to
election, had declared his purpose
to sign such bill II it were pass
ed. He mentioned the fact that
while the legislature was debating
the bill, Henry Mcbride made no
effort to aid, although he had made
his fight for the nomination on his
professed allegiance to the rail-
road commission issue. Frank T.
Post and other Mcbride commits
sion men were also absent. The
governor outlined the work Of the
commission he appointed and de-
. clared 1t the equal of any In the
, country. Jesse Jones, member
Of organized labor, Is now one OF
the commission.
